executive_summary: NO-GO con score ponderado de 40.5/100. El mercado es críticamente pequeño (SOM $15K-$120K) y dos fundamentos están por debajo de 45 (Problem 40, Market 25), activando knockouts automáticos que hacen inviable la idea en su alcance actual de queso de cabra artesanal exclusivamente en Patagonia.
key_strengths: Competitive pricing intelligence is strong (14/20) — clear pricing gap identified at ultra-low end for micro-producers under $50/mo
Revenue model validated by 5 precedents including DairyCraftPro and Craftybase serving similar artisan segments
4 thematically related market gaps identified: affordable pricing, Spanish-language support, simplified UX, and SENASA compliance
Regulatory environment is navigable (18/25) with documented compliance paths and no active enforcement actions
key_concerns: Market SOM of $15K-$120K cannot sustain a standalone business — even 100% capture would not cover one developer salary
Ultra-narrow customer segment of ~500-2,000 informal family producers with near-zero software spending history and no demonstrated willingness-to-pay
Rural Patagonia internet connectivity gap affects 40%+ of target users, making SaaS delivery fundamentally problematic
LTV/CAC ratio of 1.75x is below the 3:1 healthy threshold, and all three sensitivity scenarios (CAC+20%, churn+20%, price-20%) fail viability tests
No early adopter segments meeting all three identifiability criteria (label, spend evidence, reachable channel with 1,000+ members)
critical_assumptions: ARPU of $35/mo based on competitive positioning — but no evidence target customers would pay this amount
Monthly churn rate of 5% from SMB SaaS benchmarks — may be optimistic for informal micro-producers with seasonal cash flow
CAC of $300 assumed from agri-tech benchmarks — may be optimistic given Patagonia's geographic isolation and no established digital channels to reach producers
SOM estimate of $15K-$120K based on ~500-2,000 producers — exact producer count is uncertain; 85% are informal operations
Gross margin of 75% assumes pure cloud SaaS — offline-first requirement would increase infrastructure complexity and reduce margins

next_steps.action: Validate whether expanding scope to ALL artisan dairy producers across Argentina (not just goat cheese in Patagonia) produces a viable market — re-run Market sizing for the broader segment
next_steps.rationale: The #1 knockout is market size. Expanding geography and dairy type is the most direct way to address this. Argentina has ~11,000 registered dairy establishments vs. ~500-2,000 goat cheese producers in Patagonia alone.

next_steps.action: Conduct 10-15 direct interviews with artisan dairy producers (goat + cow + sheep) to test willingness-to-pay for inventory/traceability software at $25-50/mo price points
next_steps.rationale: Zero WTP evidence was found in online research. Before any pivot, confirm that ANY artisan dairy producer segment would pay for software. If WTP is truly zero, no pivot will work.

next_steps.action: Investigate government subsidy programs (INTA, provincial development agencies) that could fund or co-fund digitalization tools for artisan food producers — this could change the buyer from individual producer to government agency
next_steps.rationale: If individual producers cannot pay, the alternative business model is B2G (business-to-government) where agricultural agencies purchase licenses for producer cooperatives.

next_steps.action: Monitor SENASA regulatory roadmap for potential mandatory goat/sheep traceability (following bovine mandate in Jan 2026) — if mandated, this creates forced demand
next_steps.rationale: The regulatory environment is the one external factor that could fundamentally change the market dynamics. A mandate would create urgency and potentially unlock government funding for compliance tools.
